subject: Statistics
title: The Bradford Hill Criteria
description: A quick, practical guide to The Bradford Hill Criteria.

hook.text: Smoking and lung cancer were correlated. But correlation isn't causation. How did scientists prove the link?

buildup.text: In 1965, Austin Bradford Hill proposed nine criteria for judging whether an association is causal.

discovery.text: Strength, consistency, specificity, temporality, dose-response — each criterion adds evidence for causation.

twist.text: No single criterion is necessary or sufficient. It's a judgment call using all evidence together.

climax.text: The criteria convinced the world that smoking causes cancer. They remain the framework for causal reasoning in medicine.

punchline.text: Nine clues that turn correlation into causation.

quiz.question: What are the Bradford Hill criteria used for?
options: Evaluating whether an observed association between exposure and disease is likely causal
Calculating the exact probability of causation
Determining the required sample size for a clinical trial
